coredata.dc:title: Novel electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots/riboflavin nanocomposite for the detection of persulfate
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Roushani, Mahmoud
Abdi, Zeinab
coredata.dc:description: A novel and sensitive electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots/riboflavin modified glassy carbon (GC/GQDs/RF) electrode was constructed and utilized to determine persulfate (S2O8 2−). GQDs were expected to have the significant properties of graphene materials as well as new functions resulting from their quantum confinement and edge effects. The UV–vis, photoluminescence spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques were used for characterizing the GQDs. The modified electrode showed stable and well-defined redox couples at a wide pH range (1–10), with surface confined characteristics. The electron transfer coefficient (α) and the heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k s) for redox reaction of RF were found to be 0.52 and 6.59s−1, respectively. The GC/GQDs/RF electrode exhibits an excellent electrocatalytic activity for a S2O8 2− reduction, with a measured k cat of ∼4.8×103 M−1 s−1. This catalytic reduction allows an amperometric detection of S2O8 2− at a potential of −0.1V with detection limit of 0.2μM, concentration calibration range of 1.0μM to 1mM and sensitivity of 4.7nAμM−1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Persulfate
Graphene quantum dots
Riboflavin
Chemical modified electrode
Electrocatalytic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability, reproducibility and pH dependence of the GC/GQDs/RF electrode
Electrocatalytic activity of GC/GQDs/RF electrode for reduction of S2O8 2−
Amperometric detection of S2O8 2− at GC/GQDs/RF electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Cyclic voltammetric responses of a GC/GQDs/RF electrode in phosphate buffer (pH 7) at scan rates (inner to outer) of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100mVs−1. (B) and (C) plots of peak currents vs. the scan rate and square root of scan rate. (D) Variation of peak potential vs. log v.

Cyclic voltammetric response of a GC/GQDs/RF electrode at different pH (from right to left), 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 (from right to left); scan rate 50mVs−1. Inset shows the variation of standard potentials vs. pH values.

Recorded cyclic voltammograms of a GC/GQDs electrode in phosphate buffer solution (pH 2) at scan rate 25mVs−1 (a); (b) as (a) in the presence 0.2mM S2O8 2− (c and d) as (a and b) for GC/GQDs/RF electrode.

Cyclic voltammograms of GC/GQDs/RF electrode in presence different concentrations of S2O8 2− in buffer solution ((pH 2) from inner to outer 0, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100μM. at scan rate 20mVs−1. The inset shows the catalytic response vs. S2O8 2− concentrations.

Amperometric response for a rotating modified GQD/RF GC electrode at rotation speed 1000rpm and −0.1V in a pH 2 solution for successive additions of (A) 1μM and (C) 0.1mM S2O8 2−. (B) and (D) plots of chronoamperometric currents vs. S2O8 2− concentrations. (E) Recorded chronoamperogram for 0.2mM S2O8 2− during 10min.
Analytical parameters for S2O8 2− at several modified electrodes.

head.ce:title: Novel electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots/riboflavin nanocomposite for the detection of persulfate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ilam of University, Ilam, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Ilam of University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and sensitive electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots/riboflavin modified glassy carbon (GC/GQDs/RF) electrode was constructed and utilized to determine persulfate (S2O8 2−). GQDs were expected to have the significant properties of graphene materials as well as new functions resulting from their quantum confinement and edge effects. The UV–vis, photoluminescence spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) techniques were used for characterizing the GQDs. The modified electrode showed stable and well-defined redox couples at a wide pH range (1–10), with surface confined characteristics. The electron transfer coefficient (α) and the heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k s) for redox reaction of RF were found to be 0.52 and 6.59s−1, respectively. The GC/GQDs/RF electrode exhibits an excellent electrocatalytic activity for a S2O8 2− reduction, with a measured k cat of ∼4.8×103 M−1 s−1. This catalytic reduction allows an amperometric detection of S2O8 2− at a potential of −0.1V with detection limit of 0.2μM, concentration calibration range of 1.0μM to 1mM and sensitivity of 4.7nAμM−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: GQDs are one or few-layered graphene sheets with lateral dimensions smaller than 100nm [1,2]. They are different from carbon quantum dots; the latter is defined as quasi-spherical carbon particles with sizes <10nm [3]. GQDs are expected to have the significant properties due to quantum confinement and edge effects, making it an excellent candidate for construction of nanoscaled optical and electronic devices [1,4]. Thus, they have a variety of applications such as in biological imaging and labeling [5–7], light emitting diodes [8], electroluminescence [9], organic photovoltaic devices [10–12], and catalysis [13–15].

Due to stable luminescence, good biocompatibility, suitable conductivity, and low toxicity, GQDs are demonstrated to be efficient in both bioimaging and biosensing applications [5,4]. In the field of electrochemical sensors and biosensors, there has been a little attention to GQDs. Zhao et al. [16] have reported the fabrication of a sensitive biosensor based on the interaction between single-stranded DNA and GQDs.

Persulfate is a strong oxidant and has been used for many applications such as water and soil decontaminants [17], circuit board fabrication [18], cosmetics [19], and polymerization [20]. It has been reported that persulfate may either cause or intensify diseases such as asthma [21] and skin reactions [22]. Because of a gradual increase in application of persulfate finding a simple and convenient measurement of persulfate in water is desired. Therefore, many studies have been performed for the quantitative determination of persulfate in the recent years [23,24].

Riboflavin (6,7-dimethyl-9-(d-1-ribityl)-isoalloxazine), commonly called vitamin B2, is a water-soluble vitamin. RF is an essential precursor of these coenzymes. The electrochemical behavior of flavins has been studied by several investigations [25–27]. These compounds show reversible two-electron reduction and oxidation waves, both of which are due to overlapping one-electron waves.

In the present study, GQDs were used for the immobilization of riboflavin. At the first stage, GQD was simply coated on GC electrode surface by the drop casting method. Then, the GC/GQDs electrode was activated and coated with riboflavin. The modified electrode showed direct electron transfer and excellent electrocatalytic activity toward S2O8 2− reduction. The applicability of GC/GQDs/RF modified electrode as amperometric sensor for S2O8 2− detection was investigated. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the application of GC/GQDs/RF electrode for the detection of S2O8 2−.

Riboflavin, citric acid (CA), KCl, K4[Fe(CN)6] and other reagents were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and used without purification. The buffer solutions (0.1M) were made from Na3PO4, NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 and the pH was adjusted with 0.1M H3PO4 or 1.0M NaOH. The pH was measured with a Metrohm model 780 pH/mV meters (Metrohm Swiss made). Solutions were deaerated by bubbling high purity (99.99%) N2 gas through them prior to the experiments. All electrochemical experiments were carried out at a temperature of 25±0.1°C.

All the electrochemical experiments were performed on a μ-AUTOLAB type III and FRA2 board computer controlled Potentiostat/Galvanostat (Eco-Chemie, The Switzerland) driven with NOVA software. A conventional three-electrode cell was used with an Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) reference electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode, and a glassy carbon disk (modified and unmodified) as the working electrode (with diameter of 0.2cm, geometric surface area of 0.0314cm2, Metrohm Swiss made). Voltammetry on electrodes coated with GQDs and riboflavin-modified electrodes was carried out in buffers free of riboflavin molecules. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken with a Philips CM-10 TEM microscope operated at 100kV. All UV–vis absorption spectra of the sample were recorded using a VARIAN 300Bio CARY UV–vis spectrophotometer in the range of 190–800nm. Photoluminescence spectra of samples were recorded with a Carry Eclipse (Agilent) Fluorescence and Phosphorescence spectrometer.

The GQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing CA. In a typical procedure of GQDs preparation, 2g CA was put into a 5-mL beaker and heated to 200°C using a heating mantle. About 5min later, the CA was liquated. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then orange in 30min, implying the formation of GQDs. The obtained orange liquid for preparing GQDs was added drop by drop into 100mL of 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH, the aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained [28].

Prior to modification, the bare GC electrode (2mm in diameter) was polished successively with alumina on a polishing cloth and then rinsed with doubly distilled water. 3μL of GQD solution was cast on the surface of GC electrode and dried overnight at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous GC/GQDs electrode. To activate the surface of GC/GQDs electrode, pre-treatment was performed potentiostatically at 1.7V for 3min. Then the electrode was immersed in fresh phosphate solution containing 0.1mM riboflavin for 3min. A stable thin film of the riboflavin adsorbed on the electrode surfaces. The modified electrode surface was rinsed with deionized water before using. The same procedure was used for bare GC electrode in the presence riboflavin. The effective surface area of the electrode modified with GQDs was determined to be 0.1cm2 from the cyclic voltammogram of 1mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in buffer solution, pH 7.

UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy are two techniques in the assay of the as prepared GQDs. Fig. S1 (Supporting Information) shows the absorbance (curve a) and photoluminescence (curve b) of GQD. A broad UV–vis absorption with a weak shoulder at 358nm and also photoluminescence spectrum peak (under excitation at 358nm) at 456nm are characteristics of GQD according to previous reports. Also, the morphology of the prepared GQDs was studied by TEM (Fig. S2). Although high quality TEM images are difficult to be obtained due to the low electro contrast between the GQDs and the carbon coated TEM grids, some nanosheets of ∼50nm in size can still be observed.

Fig. 3S shows the cyclic voltammograms of GC/GQDs and GC/GQDs/RF electrodes in buffer solution (pH 7). As shown for the GC/GQDs electrode, no redox peaks were observed (voltammogram “a”) at a potential range of 0.2 to −0.7V, which provides a potential window of the GC/GQDs electrode for investigating the voltammetric behavior of riboflavin. By immersing the GC/GQDs modified electrode in 0.1mM riboflavin solution for 3min, a stable thin layer of riboflavin was adsorbed. As shown for GC/GQDs/RF electrode, a well-defined cyclic voltammogram with a peak potential separation of less than 20mV and a peak current of 1.5μA was observed at a formal potential of 0.41V (Fig. 3S voltammogram “b”). This result indicates that although GQD is not an excellent conductive material, high surface area of GQD, hydrophilic edges, good biocompatibility, and favorable orientation of riboflavin lead to a multivalent interaction between the GQD and coenzyme resulting in strong absorption of riboflavin on the surface of GQD that lead to a higher redox response and the reversibility of riboflavin is significantly improved. Considering low toxicity and eco-friendly properties of GQD and its function in increasing the electron transfer of riboflavin, GQD can be introduced as a new substrate for efficient coenzymes immobilization.

The electrochemical properties of GC/GQDs/RF electrode were compared with a GC electrode modified with the proposed riboflavin using physical adsorption as the modification technique. As shown in inset Fig. 3Sa, by immersing the preanodized GC electrode in 0.1mM riboflavin solution for 20min, a cyclic voltammogram with low peak current (0.04μA) was observed.

Fig. 1 A demonstrates the cyclic voltammograms of a GC electrode modified with the RF/GQD film in buffer solution pH 7, at different scan rates. Fig. 1B explains the plots of the anodic and cathodic peak current vs. the scan rate for the modified electrode. Both the anodic and cathodic peaks currents are linearly proportional to the scan rate in the range of 10–4000mVs−1, indicating a surface-confined electrode process. Moreover, redox potentials are almost the same at different scan rate, indicating that riboflavin adsorbed onto the surface undergoes a reversible electron transfer with the GQDs. The peak-to-peak separation is about 20mV at scan rates below 100mVs−1 suggesting facile charge transfer kinetics over this range of sweep rates.

According to the slope of the I p vs. v curve and the following equation the surface concentration (Γ c) of riboflavin on the surface of GC/GQDs electrode was estimated [29].
(1)
I p
=
n 2
F 2
v A
Γ c
4 R T
where v is the sweep rate, A is the effective surface area (0.1cm2) of the modified electrode, and the other symbols have their usual meaning. From the slope of cathodic peak currents vs. scan rate, the calculated surface concentration of riboflavin is 3.85×10−9 molcm−2. At higher sweep rates, the plot of peak currents vs. scan rate deviates from linearity and the peak current becomes proportional to the square root of the scan rate (Fig. 1C), indicating a diffusion-controlled process. The slow diffusion of counter ions (H+) into the electrode surfaces controlled the electrochemical reaction. At higher sweep rates (v >4000mVs−1) peak separations begin to increase, indicating the limitation due to charge transfer kinetics.

Based on Laviron theory [30], the electron transfer rate constant (k s) and charge transfer coefficient (α) can be determined by measuring the variation of peak potential with scan rate. The values of peak potentials were proportional to the logarithm of the scan rate for scan rates higher than 5Vs−1 (Fig. 1D). The slope of the ΔE c vs. log(v), was about, 62.3mV (ΔE c = E pc − E 0). Using the equation E p = K −2.3030(RT/αnF)log v and the two electrons transferred for RF, a charge transfer coefficient, α =0.52, was obtained. Introducing this α value in the following equation [30];
(2)
log
k s
= α
log ( 1 − α ) + ( 1 − α )
log
α − log
R T
n F v
− α ( 1 − α )
n F Δ E
2.3 R T
an apparent surface electron transfer rate constant, k s =6.59s−1, was estimated.
The large value of the electron transfer rate constant indicates a high ability of GQDs for promoting electrons between RF and the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and pH dependence of the GC/GQDs/RF electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the GC/GQDs/RF electrode was examined by continuous potential cycling between −0.1 and −0.7V in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7; not shown). The results indicated that after 100 repetitive cycles at a scan rate of 100mVs−1, no detectable change was observed at the peak height and potential separation. In addition, it was found that the current value is decreased only by 7% when the electrode was immersed in buffer solution (pH 7) for 24h.

The stability of the modified electrodes was evaluated by the same method in electrolyte solutions at pH 1 and 10. The results indicated that the modified electrode was stable in acidic and alkaline solution. The high stability of adsorbed riboflavin against desorption in aqueous solution is related to the chemical and mechanical stability of the GQD film and the strong adsorption of RF on the surface of GQDs.

The reproducibility of the sensor was evaluated by amperometry. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for 10 repeated measurements (in 10μM S2O8 2−) with the same GC/GQDs/RF electrode was 2.5%. For 5 GC/GQDs/RF electrodes, the RSD was 3.6%. These results indicate that the proposed sensor has excellent reproducibility.

For investigating the effect of pH on the redox response of modified electrodes, cyclic voltammograms of the GC/GQDs/RF electrode at various pH values were recorded. Fig. 2 explains that the film exhibited a single redox couple in the pH range 1<pH<10. The electrochemical behavior of GC/GQDs/RF electrode was studied at different pH using cyclic voltammetry. Anodic and cathodic peak potentials of the GC/GQDs/RF electrode were shifted to less positive values with increasing of pH. A potential pH diagram, plot of the E 0′ as a function of pH, is composed of a straight line with a slope of 55.5mV pH−1. Therefore the electro-oxidation of riboflavin obeys the Nernst equation for a two-electron and two-proton transfer reaction [31].
(3)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of GC/GQDs/RF electrode for reduction of S2O8 2−
ce:section.ce:para: Due to chemical stability, electrochemical reversibility, and high electron transfer rate constant of riboflavin at the GC/GQDs electrode, it can be used as a mediator to shuttle electron between electrodes and analyte molecules. In order to test the potential electrocatalytic activity of the GC/GQDs/RF electrode, its cyclic voltammetric responses at 25.0mVs−1 were obtained in buffer solution (pH 2.0) in the presence of 80μM riboflavin and the data are presented in Fig. 3 . Curves (a) and (c) show cyclic voltammograms of GC/GQDs electrode and GC/GQDs/RF electrode, respectively, in buffer solution without S2O8 2−. Curves (b) and (d) show the reduction peak of GC/GQDs electrode and GC/GQDs/RF electrode in the presence of S2O8 2−. For GC/GQDs electrode, no redox response for S2O8 2− reduction can be seen in the potential range from 0.10 to −0.25V (voltammogram “b”). The reducing of overvoltage and increasing the peak current of S2O8 2− reduction confirm that RF has high catalytic ability for S2O8 2− reduction. Therefore, riboflavin immobilized onto GQDs are suitable as mediators to shuttle electron between S2O8 2− and working electrode and facilitate electrochemical regeneration following electron exchange with S2O8 2−.

Cyclic voltammograms of the modified electrode in the presence different concentrations of S2O8 2− were recorded (Fig. 4 ). Inset of Fig. 4 shows the plot of catalytic current (at potential −0.1V) vs. S2O8 2− concentration. As shown, peak current linearly increased with an increase in the concentration of S2O8 2− and the calibration plot is linear (correlation coefficient, 0.998) for low concentrations (0.2–1.4μM). The sensitivity and the detection limit (3σ) of the sensor toward S2O8 2− were found to be 0.8786μA μM−1 and 1.0μM, respectively.

The cyclic voltammograms of modified electrode in buffer solution containing S2O8 2− at different scan rates were recorded (not shown). The peak current for the reduction of S2O8 2− is proportional to the square root of the scan rate, suggesting that the process be controlled by diffusion of S2O8 2− as expected for a catalytic system. It can also be noted that by increasing the sweep rate the peak potential for the catalytic reduction of S2O8 2− shifts to more negative values suggesting a kinetic limitation in the reaction between the redox sites of riboflavin and S2O8 2−. Also, a plot of the scan rate-normalized current (I p/v 1/2) vs. scan rate exhibited the characteristic shape of a typical EC′ catalytic process [32]. Based on the obtained results, the following catalytic scheme describes the reaction sequence in the reduction of S2O8 2− with riboflavin.
(4) Riboflavin (oxidized form)+2H+ +2e−
→ Riboflavin (reduced form)
(5)
Riboflavin ( reduced form ) +
S 2
O 8
2 −
⟶
k
cat
Riboflavin
( oxidized form ) + 2 S
O 4
2 −

For an EC′ mechanism, the Andriex and Saveant [33] theoretical model can be used to calculate the catalytic rate constant. Based on this theory, a relation between the peak current and the concentration of substrate for slow scan rates and large catalytic rate constant exists:
(6)
I p
= α n F A
D
1 / 2
F v
R T
1 / 2
C s
where D and C s are the diffusion coefficient (cm2 s−1) and the bulk concentration (molcm−3) of substrate (persulfate), respectively, and other symbols have their usual meanings. For low scan rates (5–20mVs−1), the average coefficient value (α) in Eq. (6) is found to be 0.3 for a GC/GQDs/RF electrode, with a surface coverage of 3.85×10−9 molcm−2 and a geometric area A of 0.1cm2 in 0.1mM persulfate at pH 2. According to the approach of Andriex and Saveant, and using Fig. 1 in Ref. [33], the average values of the calculated k cat are 4.8×103 M−1 s−1 for modified electrode. The high k cat obtained for GC/GQDs/RF electrode implies that this system can be efficiently used as electrochemical sensor for S2O8 2− detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amperometric detection of S2O8 2− at GC/GQDs/RF electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The amperometric method shows a lower background current that arises primarily from full charging the electrochemical double-layer capacitance in a short time scale, due to the fact of the potential of the working electrode is fixed during the time. Fig. 5 A shows a typical current–time plot of the rotated modified GC electrode (rotation speed 1000rpm) on successive additions of 1.0μM of persulfate at an applied potential of −0.1V (vs. Ag/AgCl). As shown, the modified electrode responded rapidly and approached 95% of the steady-state current within 3s. The plot of current response vs. S2O8 2− concentration is shown in Fig. 5B. The calibration plot is linear over a wide concentration range (1μM to 1mM), while for a high concentration of S2O8 2− the plot of current vs. analyte concentration deviates from linearity (Fig. 5D). The linear least squares calibration curve over the range of 1.0–8.0μM (8 points) is I (μA)=0.0047 [S2O8 2−]μM+0.0198μA with a correlation coefficient of 0.9903, indicating that the regression line is fitted very well with the experimental data and the regression equation can be applied in the unknown sample determination. The detection limit (when signal to noise ratio was 3) and sensitivity were 0.02μM and 4.7nAμM−1, respectively. Highly stable amperometric response toward persulfate is extremely attractive feature of the GC/GQDs/RF electrode. Fig. 5E shows the amperometric response of 0.2mM S2O8 2− during a prolonged 800s experiment. The response remains stable throughout the experiment (only a 4% decrease in current was observed), indicating that riboflavin is effectively stabilized with immobilization onto GQDs. Therefore, the modified electrode possesses excellent, stable and strong mediation properties and facilitates the low potential amperometric measurement of S2O8 2− at trace levels. The selectivity of the sensor was also evaluated by determination of S2O8 2− in the presence of common interfering ions such as, IO3 −, ClO4 −, NO2 −, SO3 2−, S2O3 2−, S2O6 2−, Ni2+, Co2+, and Zn2+. Fig. 4S shows amperometric response of the rotated modified electrode for S2O8 2− in the presence of several interference in buffer solution, pH 2. As can be seen, no response is observed for modified electrode in the presence of different interfering substances. Furthermore, the electrode response for persulfate was not changed after adding interfering substances. The selectivity and the anti interference advantages of the sensor are largely attributed to the low operating potential used in the determination.

Electron transfer rate constant, working point, detection limit and linear calibration range of the proposed modified electrode were compared with those previously reported, and the results are summarized in Table 1 . The results above demonstrate that RF/GQDs/GC electrode has satisfactory analytical performance and it can be a feasible sensor for S2O8 2−.

In this research, a GC/GQDs/RF electrode was demonstrated as a new and effective assay for obtaining an efficient sensor for electrochemical measurements of persulfate. Easy preparation of the modified electrode, also long time stability, reproducibility, high sensitivity, low detection limit and wide concentration range, have been observed. Detection limit, response time sensitivity, and linear concentration range of GC/GQDs/RF electrode were 0.2μM, 3s, 4.7nAμM−1, and 1μM–1mM, respectively. These analytical parameters are comparable or better than the results reported for S2O8 2− determination at the surface of other modified electrodes.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2014.05.054.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Large, solution-processable graphene quantum dots as light absorbers for photovoltaics

Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Formation and stabilization of palladium nanoparticles on colloidal graphene quantum dots
Graphene quantum dots-based platform for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors
Iron (II) activated persulfate oxidation of MGP contaminated soil
A simplistic printed circuit board fabrication process for course projects
Acute exposure to hair bleach causes airway hyper responsiveness in a rabbit model
Solution polymerization of acrylamide using potassium persulfate as an initiator: kinetic studies, temperature and pH dependence
Occupational asthma due to persulfate salts
Persulfate hair bleach reactions – cutaneous and respiratory manifestations
Preparation, electrochemistry, and electrocatalytic activity of lead pentacyanonitrosylferrate film immobilized on carbon ceramic electrode
Immobilization of flavine adenine dinucleotide onto nickel oxide nanostructures modified glassy carbon electrode: fabrication of highly sensitive persulfate sensor
Unusual adsorption effects in the electrochemical reduction of flavin mononucleotide at mercury electrodes
Amperometric detection of hydrogen peroxide at nano-ruthenium oxide/riboflavin nanocomposite-modified glassy carbon electrodes
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid

Voltammetric behavior of vitamin B2 on the gold electrode modified with a self-assembled monolayer of l-cysteine and its application for the determination of vitamin B2 using linear sweep stripping voltammetry
Aldehyde biosensor based on the determination of NADH enzymically generated by aldehyde dehydrogenase
Heterogeneous chemically modified electrodes, polymer electrodes vs. homogeneous catalysis of electrochemical reactions

Voltammetric determination of persulfate anions using an electrode modified with a Prussian blue film
Poly(brilliant cresyl blue) electrodeposited on multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified electrode and its application for persulfate determination
High sensitivity amperometric and voltammetric determination of persulfate with neutral red/nickel oxide nanowires modified carbon paste electrodes

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mahmoud Roushani received his MSc degree in analytical chemistry from University of Kurdistan, Iran (2006). He completed PhD degree in analytical chemistry at Razi University in 2011. Currently he is working as a faculty member at University of Ilam. His main research interest is fabrication of nanomaterials and its application to construction of sensors and biosensors.

Zeinab Abdi received BSc and MSc degrees in Chemistry and Analytical Chemistry in 2010 and 2013, respectively from University of Ilam, Ilam, Iran. Her topic research in MSc was about novel nanomaterials for biosensors.
